epigraph.text: The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork.

content.text:
The pyramids of Egypt stand as monuments to humanity's earliest 
achievements in geometry and engineering. These massive structures, 
built over four thousand years ago, demonstrate the practical 
application of geometric principles we will explore in this section. 
From the base of each pyramid, four triangular faces rise to meet 
at a single point—the apex, reaching toward the heavens.

In this section, we study pyramids and their circular counterpart, 
cones. Both share a distinctive feature: lateral surfaces that 
converge to a single point called the *apex* or *vertex*. 
This convergence leads to an important relationship—the volumes 
of pyramids and cones are exactly one-third the volume of corresponding 
prisms and cylinders with the same base and height.
Pyramids: Definition and Classification

content.title: Pyramid
content.content:
A **pyramid** is a polyhedron consisting of a 
polygonal **base** and triangular 
**lateral faces** that meet 
at a common point called the **apex** (or **vertex**).

content.text: Like prisms, pyramids are named according to the shape of their bases:

content.title: Height and Slant Height of a Pyramid
content.content:
The **height** (or **altitude**) 
of a pyramid is the perpendicular distance from the apex to the 
plane of the base.

For a **regular pyramid** (one whose 
base is a regular polygon and whose apex lies directly above the 
center of the base), the **slant height**, 
denoted $\ell$, is the height of any lateral face, measured from 
the apex to the midpoint of a base edge.

content.text:
For a regular pyramid with base edge $s$, height $h$, and apothem 
$a$ (the distance from the center of the base to the midpoint of 
a side), the slant height is related to these quantities by the 
Pythagorean Theorem:
\[
\ell^2 = h^2 + a^2
\]
Lateral Area and Surface Area of Regular Pyramids

For a regular pyramid, all lateral faces are congruent isosceles 
triangles. If the base has $n$ sides each of length $s$, and the 
slant height is $\ell$, then each lateral face has area 
$\frac{1}{2}s\ell$.

content.title: Lateral and Surface Area of a Regular Pyramid
content.content:
Let a regular pyramid have base perimeter $P$, base area $B$, and 
slant height $\ell$. Then:

- **Lateral Area:** $L = \frac{1}{2}P\ell$

- **Surface Area:** $S = L + B = \frac{1}{2}P\ell + B$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If the base is a regular $n$-gon with side length $s$, then:

- Each of the $n$ lateral faces is an isosceles triangle 
 with base $s$ and height $\ell$ (the slant height).

- The area of each lateral face is $\frac{1}{2}s\ell$.

- The total lateral area is $n \cdot \frac{1}{2}s\ell = 
 \frac{1}{2}(ns)\ell = \frac{1}{2}P\ell$.
Adding the base: $S = \frac{1}{2}P\ell + B$.

content.title: Surface area of a square pyramid
content.solution:
The base perimeter is $P = 4 \times 10 = 40$ cm.

The base area is $B = 10^2 = 100$ cm$^2$.

The lateral area is:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}P\ell = \frac{1}{2}(40)(13) = 260 \text{ cm}^2
\]

The surface area is:
\[
S = L + B = 260 + 100 = 360 \text{ cm}^2
\]

content.title: Finding slant height
content.problem:
A regular square pyramid has base edge 8 m and height 9 m. Find 
the slant height and the lateral surface area.
content.solution:
For a square pyramid, the apothem of the base equals half the 
side length: $a = 4$ m.

Using the Pythagorean Theorem with the right triangle formed by 
the height, apothem, and slant height:
\[
\ell^2 = h^2 + a^2 = 9^2 + 4^2 = 81 + 16 = 97
\]
\[
\ell = \sqrt{97} \approx 9.85 \text{ m}
\]

The perimeter is $P = 4 \times 8 = 32$ m.

The lateral area is:
\[
L = \frac{1}{2}P\ell = \frac{1}{2}(32)(\sqrt{97}) = 16\sqrt{97} \approx 157.6 \text{ m}^2
\]

content.text: Volume of a Pyramid

One of the most remarkable results in solid geometry is that the 
volume of a pyramid is exactly one-third the volume of a prism 
with the same base and height. This relationship was known to the 
ancient Egyptians empirically and was proved rigorously by Eudoxus 
around 400 BCE.

content.title: Volume of a Pyramid
content.content:
The volume of a pyramid with base area $B$ and height $h$ is:
\[
V = \frac{1}{3}Bh
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
*Why one-third?* One elegant proof involves dissecting a 
cube into three congruent pyramids. Consider a cube with vertices 
at the origin. Three pyramids, each with one face of the cube as 
base and the center of the cube as apex, exactly fill the cube. 
Since they are congruent, each has volume $\frac{1}{3}$ of the cube.

By Cavalieri's Principle, this one-third relationship holds for 
*all* pyramids, not just regular ones.

{3}$ appears because a pyramid 
``tapers'' uniformly from base to apex. The cross-sectional area 
at height $x$ is $B(1-x/h)^2$, and integrating this from $0$ to $h$ 
gives $$\frac{1}{3}$Bh$.}

content.title: Volume of a square pyramid
content.problem:
The Great Pyramid of Giza originally had a square base with side 
length approximately 230 meters and a height of 147 meters. Find 
its volume.
content.solution:
The base area is:
\[
B = 230^2 = 52{,}900 \text{ m}^2
\]

The volume is:
\[
V = \frac{1}{3}Bh = \frac{1}{3}(52{,}900)(147) = \frac{7{,}776{,}300}{3} = 2{,}592{,}100 \text{ m}^3
\]

This is approximately 2.6 million cubic meters!

content.title: Volume of a triangular pyramid
content.problem:
Find the volume of a pyramid whose base is an equilateral triangle 
with side length 6 and whose height is 8.
content.solution:
The area of an equilateral triangle with side $s$ is:
\[
B = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}s^2 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}(36) = 9\sqrt{3}
\]

The volume is:
\[
V = \frac{1}{3}Bh = \frac{1}{3}(9\sqrt{3})(8) = 24\sqrt{3} \approx 41.6
\]

content.text: Cones

Just as a cylinder is a "circular prism," a cone is a "circular 
pyramid"—a pyramid with a circular base.

content.title: Cone
content.content:
A **cone** is a solid with a circular base and a 
lateral surface that narrows smoothly to a point called the 
**apex** (or **vertex**). The 
**axis** is the segment from the apex to 
the center of the base.

content.title: Right Cone
content.content:
A **right cone** is a cone whose axis is 
perpendicular to the base. The **slant height**, 
denoted $\ell$, is the distance from the apex to any point on the 
edge of the base.

content.text:
For a right cone, the relationship between radius $r$, height $h$, 
and slant height $\ell$ is given by the Pythagorean Theorem:
\[
\ell^2 = r^2 + h^2
\]
Lateral and Surface Area of a Right Cone

If we "unroll" the lateral surface of a cone, we obtain a sector 
of a circle. This sector has radius equal to the slant height $\ell$ 
and arc length equal to the circumference of the base, $2\pi r$.

The area of a sector with radius $\ell$ and arc length $s$ is 
$\frac{1}{2}\ell s$. Since the arc length is $2\pi r$:

content.title: Lateral and Surface Area of a Right Cone
content.content:
The lateral area and surface area of a right cone with radius $r$ 
and slant height $\ell$ are:

- **Lateral Area:** $L = \pi r\ell$

- **Surface Area:** $S = \pi r\ell + \pi r^2 = \pi r(\ell + r)$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Surface area of a cone
content.problem: Find the surface area of a cone with radius 6 cm and slant height 10 cm.
content.solution:
The lateral area is:
\[
L = \pi r\ell = \pi(6)(10) = 60\pi \text{ cm}^2
\]

The base area is:
\[
B = \pi r^2 = \pi(36) = 36\pi \text{ cm}^2
\]

The surface area is:
\[
S = L + B = 60\pi + 36\pi = 96\pi \text{ cm}^2 \approx 301.6 \text{ cm}^2
\]

content.title: Finding slant height from height
content.problem:
A cone has radius 5 m and height 12 m. Find the slant height and 
lateral surface area.
content.solution:
Using the Pythagorean Theorem:
\[
\ell = \sqrt{r^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13 \text{ m}
\]

The lateral area is:
\[
L = \pi r\ell = \pi(5)(13) = 65\pi \text{ m}^2 \approx 204.2 \text{ m}^2
\]

content.text: Volume of a Cone

Since a cone is a "circular pyramid," its volume is one-third 
the volume of a cylinder with the same base and height.

content.title: Volume of a Cone
content.content:
The volume of a cone with radius $r$ and height $h$ is:
\[
V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
By Cavalieri's Principle, this formula applies to both right and 
oblique cones.

content.title: Volume of a cone
content.problem: Find the volume of a cone with radius 6 and height 10.

content.title: Finding height from volume
content.problem:
A conical tank has volume $48\pi$ cubic feet and radius 4 feet. 
Find its height.
content.solution:
Let $h$ be the height. Using $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h$:
$$
48\pi &= \frac{1}{3}\pi(16)h \\
48\pi &= \frac{16\pi h}{3} \\
48 &= \frac{16h}{3} \\
144 &= 16h \\
h &= 9 \text{ feet}
$$

content.title: Comparing cone and cylinder
content.problem:
A cone and a cylinder have the same radius (5 cm) and the same 
height (12 cm). How do their volumes compare?
content.solution:
Volume of the cylinder:
\[
V_{\text{cyl}} = \pi r^2 h = \pi(25)(12) = 300\pi \text{ cm}^3
\]

Volume of the cone:
\[
V_{\text{cone}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h = \frac{1}{3}(300\pi) = 100\pi \text{ cm}^3
\]

The cone has exactly one-third the volume of the cylinder.

content.title: Ice cream cone problem
content.problem:
An ice cream cone has diameter 6 cm and height 12 cm. A spherical 
scoop of ice cream with diameter 6 cm is placed on top. If the ice 
cream melts completely into the cone, will it overflow?
content.solution:
The cone has radius 3 cm and height 12 cm:
\[
V_{\text{cone}} = \frac{1}{3}\pi(9)(12) = 36\pi \text{ cm}^3
\]

The sphere has radius 3 cm:
\[
V_{\text{sphere}} = \frac{4}{3}\pi(27) = 36\pi \text{ cm}^3
\]

The volumes are equal! The melted ice cream will exactly fill the 
cone without overflowing.

content.text: Frustums

content.title: Frustum
content.content:
A **frustum** of a cone or pyramid is the 
portion remaining when the top is cut off by a plane parallel 
to the base.

content.title: Volume of a Frustum of a Cone
content.content:
The volume of a frustum of a cone with height $h$, lower base 
radius $R$, and upper base radius $r$ is:
\[
V = \frac{1}{3}\pi h(R^2 + Rr + r^2)
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Volume of a frustum
content.problem:
A frustum of a cone has height 6, lower radius 8, and upper radius 4. 
Find its volume.

content.text: Exercises

marginNotes.text:
Then they said, "Come, let us build 
ourselves a city and a tower with its top in the heavens."

By wisdom a house is built, and 
by understanding it is established; by knowledge the rooms are 
filled with all precious and pleasant riches.

Everyone then who hears these words 
of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house 
on the rock.

Behold, I am the one who has laid 
as a foundation in Zion, a stone, a tested stone, a precious 
cornerstone, of a sure foundation.

A false balance is an abomination 
to the Lord, but a just weight is his delight.
